questions.question: What is the primary purpose of computer forensics?
options: To recover deleted files for personal use
To investigate crimes by collecting, analyzing and presenting digital evidence
To remove malware from infected systems
questions.description: Computer forensics is the application of science and technology to investigate crimes and gather digital evidence for legal proceedings.

questions.question: How many steps are in the standard process of computer forensics?
questions.description: The six-step process is: Identification, Preservation, Extraction, Documentation, Interpretation, and Presentation.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the six steps of computer forensics?
questions.description: Encryption is not part of the forensic process. The six steps are Identification, Preservation, Extraction, Documentation, Interpretation, and Presentation.

questions.question: What is chain of custody?
options: A physical chain securing crime scene devices
A documented record of everyone who handled evidence and when
questions.description: Chain of custody is the documented chronological record tracking all individuals who have handled or accessed evidence.

questions.question: What is the primary cyber law in India addressing digital crimes?
questions.description: The IT Act 2000 is India's main legislation governing cyber crimes and digital offenses.

questions.question: What happens if chain of custody is broken?
options: Evidence may become inadmissible in court
questions.description: A broken chain raises integrity questions and courts may reject the evidence as inadmissible.

questions.question: Which phase of forensics focuses on preventing any changes to evidence?
questions.description: Preservation specifically maintains evidence integrity and prevents any modifications after collection.

questions.question: What does the Extraction phase involve?
options: Copying and recovering data from storage media
questions.description: Extraction involves copying and recovering actual data from storage devices for further analysis.

questions.question: What is the key difference between a Computer Crime and an Unauthorized Activity?
options: Computer crimes carry legal penalties; unauthorized activities may only violate organizational policies
questions.description: Computer crimes are prosecutable offenses under law, while unauthorized activities may only breach company policy with no criminal liability.

questions.question: Why must Identification always precede Preservation in the forensic process?
options: It doesn't matter - steps can be reordered
You must first locate and recognize evidence before you can protect it from modification
Identification is the least important step
questions.description: Without identifying what constitutes evidence, investigators cannot know what needs to be preserved.

questions.question: Why is Documentation critical throughout the entire investigation, not just at the end?
options: It is only needed for the final report
Real-time documentation creates an unbroken audit trail supporting legal admissibility and peer review
Documentation is optional for experienced investigators
It only matters for digital evidence
questions.description: Contemporaneous documentation ensures that every action is recorded, making findings legally defensible and independently verifiable.

questions.question: What makes digital evidence legally admissible in court?
options: Integrity, authenticity, unbroken chain of custody, and proper forensic procedures
The size of the evidence file
questions.description: Courts require proof that evidence is genuine, unaltered, and properly handled throughout its lifecycle.

questions.question: An investigator re-images an already-collected drive without documenting the action. Which forensic principle is violated, and what is the likely legal consequence?
options: Both the integrity principle and chain of custody are violated; the evidence may be deemed tampered with and inadmissible
Only the efficiency principle is affected
questions.description: Undocumented actions break chain of custody and raise tampering concerns - courts may exclude the evidence entirely.

questions.question: How can a technically valid forensic investigation still fail to secure a conviction?
options: Poor documentation, broken chain of custody, or improper interpretation can make valid evidence legally unusable
Convictions are always guaranteed with good evidence
Technical validity has no effect on legal outcomes
questions.description: Legal admissibility requires procedural compliance, not just technical accuracy - gaps in documentation or custody break the evidential chain.

questions.question: Why does the Interpretation phase require both technical expertise AND objectivity?
options: Interpretation is straightforward and unbiased by nature
Investigators must translate raw data into findings without introducing personal bias, since misinterpretation can lead to wrongful conclusions in court
questions.description: Forensic interpretation can be challenged in court; bias or error in analysis can misdirect investigations and harm innocent parties.

questions.question: What are the three core components of computer forensics involvement?
questions.description: Effective forensics requires people (skilled investigators), processes (documented methodology), and technology (appropriate tools).

questions.question: What is the primary goal of forensic analysis?
options: To gather digital evidence in a manner that maintains integrity for legal proceedings
questions.description: Forensic analysis focuses on gathering legally admissible evidence that can support prosecution.

questions.question: What does Live Forensics examine?
options: Running systems to capture volatile data that exists only in RAM
questions.description: Live forensics captures volatile memory (RAM) contents, active connections, and running processes before shutdown destroys them.

questions.question: What is Dead Forensics focused on?
options: Examining systems that have crashed permanently
Analyzing powered-down systems for persistent storage evidence
questions.description: Dead forensics examines storage devices (HDDs, SSDs, USBs) that retain data even when the power is off.

questions.question: What should an Incident Response Plan always include?
options: Roles, responsibilities, procedures, communication plans, and recovery strategies
Just a list of backup servers
questions.description: A comprehensive IR plan covers all aspects of response from roles to communication and full recovery.

questions.question: Who typically forms an effective Incident Response Team?
options: Forensic experts, IT staff, management, legal counsel, and communications specialists
questions.description: Effective incident response requires diverse expertise; legal and communications roles are as important as technical ones.

questions.question: What is the primary concern when detecting incidents?
options: Early recognition of unauthorized activities to enable timely containment
questions.description: Early detection minimizes damage, limits data loss, and preserves more evidence for investigation.

questions.question: What does the Preparation phase of incident response involve?
options: Waiting for an incident to occur before planning
Proactively establishing tools, training teams, and defining procedures before incidents happen
Only writing documentation after an incident
questions.description: Preparation ensures that when incidents occur, teams have the tools, skills, and procedures to respond effectively.

questions.question: Why is volatile data critical in Live Forensics?
options: Volatile data is less important than stored data
RAM, running processes, and network connections are lost forever when power is cut - they can reveal active attacks
It is easier to collect than disk data
questions.description: Volatile data provides a real-time snapshot of an active compromise and is permanently destroyed on shutdown.

questions.question: How does Network Forensics differ from traditional disk forensics?
options: Network forensics analyzes traffic, logs, and flows across infrastructure; disk forensics examines stored data on individual devices
questions.description: Different evidence types require different tools and techniques; network evidence is transient while disk evidence is persistent.

questions.question: What must organizations do BEFORE an incident occurs to support effective forensic investigation?
options: Establish baselines, implement logging, create backups, train staff, and set up secure evidence storage
questions.description: Pre-incident preparation dramatically improves detection speed, response effectiveness, and evidence quality.

questions.question: Why should the Incident Response Team include legal counsel?
options: Legal counsel ensures evidence is handled in compliance with laws and that investigation actions don't expose the organization to liability
questions.description: Legal guidance during response prevents procedural errors that could compromise prosecution or create new legal exposure.

questions.question: What is the critical difference between an Incident Response Plan and an Incident Response Procedure?
options: A plan defines strategy and roles; procedures define step-by-step actions for specific incident types
Plans are only for major incidents
questions.description: Plans provide strategic framework; procedures provide tactical execution guides for specific scenarios.

questions.question: What tension exists between rapid incident containment and forensic evidence preservation, and how should it be resolved?
options: Design response procedures that enable containment while preserving evidence - document actions taken before containment changes the system state
Evidence collection always takes absolute priority
Containment and evidence are incompatible goals
questions.description: IR must balance both goals: contain the threat to limit damage while documenting system state before any changes are made.

questions.question: Why might an organization with excellent Live Forensics capabilities still miss critical evidence in an incident?
options: If volatile data was not captured at exactly the right moment, or if the attacker cleared logs, critical evidence may have already been destroyed
questions.description: Volatile evidence is time-sensitive; delayed response or deliberate log clearing means evidence is irretrievably lost.

questions.question: What is the purpose of an Evidence Checkout Log?
options: To track who accesses evidence and when, maintaining complete accountability
To list all crime scene items
questions.description: Checkout logs provide a detailed access history for each piece of evidence, supporting chain of custody.

questions.question: What is forensic duplication?
options: Creating a bit-for-bit exact copy of storage media with cryptographic verification for analysis
questions.description: Forensic duplication creates an exact copy verified by hash values so that the original evidence remains unexamined.

questions.question: What is the FIRST action at a digital crime scene?
options: Power on all computers to check them
Secure the scene, restrict access, and document the environment before touching anything
questions.description: Securing the scene prevents contamination and unauthorized access before any evidence is collected.

questions.question: How is digital evidence authenticated after duplication?
options: By comparing cryptographic hash values of the original and copy
By counting the number of files
questions.description: Hash verification (MD5, SHA-256) proves a copy is bit-for-bit identical to the original.

questions.question: What is the most common evidence handling mistake that renders evidence inadmissible?
options: Failing to document evidence handling or breaking the chain of custody
questions.description: Documentation failures and chain of custody breaks are the most common reasons evidence is rejected by courts.

questions.question: How should digital evidence be physically stored?
options: In a secured, access-logged, climate-controlled facility protected from electromagnetic interference
Storage environment doesn't affect digital evidence
questions.description: Digital media is sensitive to temperature, humidity, and electromagnetic fields; improper storage can destroy evidence.

questions.question: What does forensic Detection identify?
options: Indicators of unauthorized activity or anomalies hidden within evidence
questions.description: Detection in forensic context means identifying the specific artifacts and indicators of compromise within collected evidence.

questions.question: What makes forensic duplication fundamentally different from a regular system backup?
options: Forensic duplication is bit-exact, includes unallocated space, deleted data, and is cryptographically verified; backups only copy active files
questions.description: Forensic duplication captures ALL data including deleted files, slack space, and unallocated areas that backups skip.

questions.question: Why is formulating a Response Strategy BEFORE examining evidence important?
options: It defines what to look for, prevents unfocused 'forensic fishing', and ensures analysis aligns with the investigation's legal objectives
It is only required by management
Strategy is less important than technical skill
questions.description: Without clear objectives, investigators may waste time or miss key evidence; strategy focuses efforts and shows professional methodology to courts.

questions.question: How does an Evidence Checkout Log contribute to court admissibility?
options: It has no impact on admissibility
It provides proof of controlled access, demonstrating that evidence integrity was maintained throughout its custody
questions.description: Courts require documented evidence of who had access; without this, defense attorneys can argue tampering occurred.

questions.question: What must investigators do if they discover evidence has been encrypted during analysis?
options: Document the discovery, update the response strategy, seek legal authority if needed, and apply decryption resources
questions.description: Encrypted evidence may indicate deliberate concealment and requires updated strategy, possibly including legal tools for decryption.

questions.question: How can consistent evidence handling protocols across all phases - Collection, Storage, Analysis, and Presentation - ensure courtroom admissibility?
options: Every phase must maintain integrity standards with documentation; a single break in any phase creates a gap that defense attorneys can exploit
Presentation phase is the only legally critical phase
questions.description: Courts evaluate the entire evidence lifecycle - any gap in handling or documentation across any phase can be used to challenge admissibility.

questions.question: Why might forensically sound duplication still fail to capture all relevant evidence?
options: Steganography, encryption, anti-forensic tools, or evidence stored outside the duplicated media may all remain undetected
All evidence is always on the primary drive
questions.description: Sophisticated attackers use anti-forensic techniques; complete evidence capture requires analysis beyond bit-level duplication.

questions.question: What is hexadecimal notation?
options: A base-16 numbering system using digits 0-9 and letters A-F
questions.description: Hexadecimal (base-16) provides compact representation of binary data using 0-9 and A-F.

questions.question: What is the hexadecimal equivalent of decimal 255?
questions.description: 255 = (15 × 16) + 15 = FF in hexadecimal.

questions.question: What does the '0x' prefix indicate in forensic tool output?
options: That the following value is in hexadecimal format
questions.description: '0x' is the standard programming and forensic convention indicating hexadecimal notation.

questions.question: How many bits does a single hexadecimal digit represent?
questions.description: Each hex digit represents exactly 4 bits (2^4 = 16 possible values per hex digit).

questions.question: What is the hex representation of binary 11010110?
questions.description: Split into nibbles: 1101 = D (13), 0110 = 6 → D6 in hexadecimal.

questions.question: Why is understanding hexadecimal important for a forensic investigator?
options: It is not important in modern forensics
All forensic tools display raw data in hexadecimal; investigators must read it to interpret evidence directly
Only software developers need hex knowledge
Hexadecimal is only used in networking
questions.description: Forensic tools represent all data in hex; investigators who cannot read hex cannot interpret raw evidence.

questions.question: What should an Initial Assessment evaluate first?
options: The scope, nature, scale, and evidence types of the incident
questions.description: Initial assessment informs all subsequent investigation decisions by understanding the full scope of the incident.
